

Clustering Project Report  
Sanchit Bhadani  
134371  
3/18/2025  
**Dataset Description**  
**Data Source**  
The source used was obtained from:  
[https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/arjunbhasin2013/ccdata](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/arjunbhasin2013/ccdata)

The dataset is called “Credit Card Dataset for Clustering”.  
The shape is 8950 rows by 18 columns, but after removing the unnecessary features, the shape was 8950 rows by 14 columns.

![][image1]  
     (Before removing                                 		    (After removing  
  Unnecessary features) 	 	                        unnecessary features)

The clustering objective is to determine what credit card users are high spenders, credit dependent users, and low activity users.

**Features Table**

| Feature | Type | Description | Range / Values |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| CUST\_ID | Categorical (ID) | Unique identifier for each customer | Alphanumeric string |
| BALANCE | Numeric | Current credit card balance | 0 – \~19,000 |
| BALANCE\_FREQUENCY | Numeric | Frequency the balance is updated | 0 – 1 |
| PURCHASES | Numeric | Total purchase amount on the card | 0 – \~50,000 |
| ONEOFF\_PURCHASES | Numeric | Value of large one-time purchases | 0 – \~40,000 |
| INSTALLMENTS\_PURCHASES | Numeric | Value of purchases made in installments | 0 – \~22,000 |
| CASH\_ADVANCE | Numeric | Total cash withdrawn using the card | 0 – \~47,000 |
| PURCHASES\_FREQUENCY | Numeric | Frequency of purchase activity | 0 – 1 |
| ONEOFF\_PURCHASES\_FREQUENCY | Numeric | Frequency of one-time purchases | 0 – 1 |
| PURCHASES\_INSTALLMENTS\_FREQUENCY | Numeric | Frequency of installment purchases | 0 – 1 |
| CASH\_ADVANCE\_FREQUENCY | Numeric | Frequency of cash advances | 0 – 1 |
| CASH\_ADVANCE\_TRX | Numeric | Number of cash advance transactions | 0 – \~120 |
| PURCHASES\_TRX | Numeric | Number of purchase transactions | 0 – \~350 |
| CREDIT\_LIMIT | Numeric | Maximum credit limit on the card | \~50 – \~30,000 |
| PAYMENTS | Numeric | Total payments made by the customer | 0 – \~50,000 |
| MINIMUM\_PAYMENTS | Numeric | Minimum payment amounts made | 0 – \~75,000 |
| PRC\_FULL\_PAYMENT | Numeric | Percent of time full balance is paid | 0 – 1 |
| TENURE | Numeric | Months the account has been active | 6 – 12 |

**Clustering Objective**

The goal of the project is to identify distinct groups of credit card users based on their financial behavior, including spending patterns, payment habits, credit usage, and transaction frequency. By applying clustering techniques, the analysis will show groups of customers who carry the same qualities of credit card usage based on their user data. Clustering is useful for this dataset since the goal is not to predict a target variable, but rather group users based on their credit card spending habits. Although the dataset does not contain predefined category labels, the clusters made from the AI machine learning algorithm can be interpreted into groups of users that represent high spenders, customers that rely heavily on cash advances, or even users that consistently pay their balances in full rather than installments.  
**Data Suitability**

The dataset used is well-suited for clustering because it consists of entirely numeric features except for the categorical variable CUST\_ID. However, because CUST\_ID provides no value in determining customer behavior and is only a marker to identify customers, the variable was dropped before training the machine learning algorithm. Therefore, the only features that were used were numerical, so no encoding was required. The four features excluded from the dataset for the analysis section are ONEOFF\_PURCHASES, PURCHASES\_INSTALLMENTS\_FREQUENCY, CASH\_ADVANCE\_TRX, and CUST\_ID. Their features were excluded because they had a very high correlation with a variable that measured a similar behavior. In total, 14 features were used for the analysis, which proved to be a reasonable number of features used for the analysis. In total, around 8950 customers were used as samples for the dataset. Because of the high number of features and samples, the dataset is credible and representative of a wider population.

**Exploratory Data Analysis**

**Summary Statistics**

![][image3]  
![][image4]

**Visualizations**

Because the dataset contains 14 features, the top 5 were chosen based on how high their impact was on determining clusters of users. The top 5 that were chosen backed by research were BALANCE, PURCHASES, CASH\_ADVANCE, CREDIT\_LIMIT, and PRC\_FULL\_PAYMENT.

BALANCE  
![][image5]

PURCHASES  
![][image6]

CASH\_ADVANCE  
![][image7]

CREDIT\_LIMIT  
![][image8]

PRC\_FULL\_PAYMENT  
![][image9]

Scatterplot Showing Likewise Relationships

Similarly, due to the high amount of features, scatterplot comparisons were only made for the 8 highest impacting features.

Title is “Pairwise Relationships of All Selected Features (Log Transformed)”  
![][image10]![][image11]

Correlational Heatmap of Features  
![][image12]

Because there were no categorical variables in the list of features used for analysis, no bar charts were needed.

**Key Insights**

The data analysis revealed several key patterns in customer behavior, including high variation and right-skewness in spending-related features such as purchases and cash advances. Strong correlations were observed among certain purchase variables, while other features showed weak relationships, indicating diverse and independent behavioral patterns. Additionally, scatter plots suggested potential natural groupings of customers, and significant outliers were identified, representing high-value or heavy credit users. Variables that had high correlation were excluded from the analysis to increase diversity in variables and prevent redundancy in data which could weigh the data incorrectly. By doing this, variables were not counted twice during the clustering allowing for more accurate machine learning. Some potential groupings are high spenders, low activity users, and users dependent on loans or cash advances. Several features, including purchases, cash advances, and payments, showed a clear presence of outliers, as shown by the box plot. The outliers were retained since they reflect categories of customers such as high activity or high value customers, which should be shown when the clustering analysis is shown. As a result, the final decision was to keep the outliers.

 **Data Preprocessing**

**Missing Values**

				                To deal with the missing values, the missing value in the 	  
					    CREDIT\_LIMIT column was filled in with the mean of C					    CREDIT\_LIMIT, and the missing 313 values from                            
					    MINIMUM\_PAYMENTS were filled in with the mean     
					    of MINIMUM\_PAYMENTS. This allowed for the most    
                                                                accurate and reasonable handling of missing values.

**Duplicate Rows**

                                                         Because no rows were duplicated, no action was required to   
				         handle duplicated rows.

**Outliers**

Outliers were detected using IQR, where outliers were 1.5 \* IQR less than Q1 or 1.5\* IQR more than Q3. The table to the left shows the number of outliers in each column. Ultimately the outliers were kept because they represented groups that had abnormally large amounts of activity or credit card usage, which could possibly represent a cluster and should be equally represented.

**Features**  
The features that were used in the final analysis were BALANCE, BALANCE\_FREQUENCY, PURCHASES, INSTALLMENTS\_PURCHASES, CASH\_ADVANCE, PURCHASES\_FREQUENCY, ONEOFF\_PURCHASES\_FREQUENCY, CASH\_ADVANCE\_FREQUENCY, PURCHASES\_TRX, CREDIT\_LIMIT, PAYMENTS, MINIMUM\_PAYMENTS, PRC\_FULL\_PAYMENT, and TENURE.

The CUST\_ID column was removed because it is a unique identifier and does not contribute to clustering. Additionally, ONEOFF\_PURCHASES, PURCHASES\_INSTALLMENTS\_FREQUENCY, and CASH\_ADVANCE\_TRX were excluded due to strong correlations with other features, indicating redundancy. Removing these variables helps reduce duplication of information and improves the effectiveness of distance-based clustering algorithms. No features were engineered and added.

**Encoding**  
Because no categorical variables were used, encoding was not needed to process this dataset.

**Feature Scaling**  
The features were scaled using StandardScaler. All numeric features within the final features list were scaled (which means all of them). StandardScaler was used rather than MinMax Scaler because the dataset being used contains a large number of outliers. MinMax scaling requires datasets that are clustered together with no outliers at all. If MinMax scaling was used, then most data would lose its detail because almost all the values would get squished near 0\. With StandardScaler, every datapoint is equally represented.

**Clustering Algorithms**

**Optimal K Determination**  
To determine the optimal number of clusters, the Elbow Method was applied for the K-Means clustering. With the WCSS (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares) as the metric being determined, each K value was tested for and analyzed.  
![][image13]  
The resulting analysis showed a clear inflection point at k \= 4, where the reduction in the inertia value starts decreasing at a lower rate. This shows that increased number of clusters after k \= 4 do not significantly impact the accuracy of the clusters. Therefore, k \= 4 was the optimal number of clusters.

For hierarchical clustering, using the ward linkage specifically, the dendrogram visually shows how data points are being clustered based on distance. 

![][image14]  
After analyzing the dendrogram, a significant increase in the vertical distance between clusters when moving from 4 clusters to fewer clusters was observed. This means that fewer clusters than 4 clusters would cluster together data points that were not similar, whereas more than 4 clusters would be too specific and result in over-segmentation of the data. Therefore, selecting 4 clusters provides the best balance between cluster separation and cohesion.

**K-Means Implementation**  
The K-Means clustering model was trained with 4 clusters (value of k was 4), as well as a random state set at 42\. This ensures reproducibility of the training model. The max iterations documented was 300, with a n\_init value of 10\. The cluster sizes produced were 3209, 4175, 1257, and 309 for groups 0, 1, 2, and 3 respectively.

![][image15]![][image16]

**Hierarchical Implementation**  
The linkage method used for the hierarchical clustering was Ward, which minimizes the variance within clusters and is much better suited for numeric, standardized data when compared to the other linkage methods. Based on the dendrogram, the number of clusters was set to k \= 4, since 4 clusters provided the most meaningful separation between groups. The cluster sizes are listed below:  
![][image17]![][image18]

**Algorithm Parameters**  
K-Means clustering involved numerous parameters. The main parameters were n\_clusters \= 4, random\_state \= 42, max\_iter \= 300, and n\_init \= 10\. The value of k \= 4 was based off of the Elbow Method model, the random\_state was set to ensure reproducibility, max\_iter was set to 300 to allow for enough iterations for convergence without making the model take too long to train, and the n\_init \= 10 parameter was set to run the algorithm multiple times with different centroid initializations and select the best result, allowing for improved stability and the best clustering of the data.

For hierarchical clustering, the key parameters were linkage \= ‘ward’, set to ward since it was an appropriate linkage choice since it best suited datasets with mostly or all numerical features, reducing within-cluster variance. The n\_clusters \= 4 parameter was used because an analysis of the dendrogram showed that using 4 clusters provided the best segmentation of the dataset.

**Evaluation & Results**

**Performance Metrics**  
Because the dataset did not contain pre-labeled clusters, the ARI could not be calculated. For the K-Means clustering, the WCSS (or inertia) value was 83221.74. Below is a performance comparison chart:

| Algorithm | Clusters (k) | ARI Score | Inertia (WCSS) | Silhouette Score |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| K-Means | 4 | N/A | 83221.748712 | 0.180747 |
| Hierarchial | 4 | N/A | N/A | 0.187291 |

**Cluster Visualizations**  
K-Means 2D Scatterplot  
![][image19]

Elbow Curve Graph:  
![][image20]

Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram:  
![][image21]

Hierarchical Clustering Scatterplot:  
![][image22]

Due to the dataset not being pre-labeled, a predicted vs actual comparison cannot be made.

**Best Algorithm Selection**  
Based on the previous evaluation metrics and visualizations, the hierarchical clustering model was the best algorithm. When tested, it had the highest silhouette score at k \= 4 and showed a clearer distinction between clusters than the K-Means clustering algorithm on the scatterplot. It was also much more consistent in its silhouette scores than the K-Means model was. This algorithm works best for this data because it assimilates data points that share certain features and works its way up, with longer distances showing further segmentation or increased differences between two data points or groups of data points.  
**Cluster Interpretation**

**Cluster Characteristics**  
For K-Means, the sizes of each cluster are shown below:  
![][image23]

For Hierarchical clustering, the sizes of each cluster are shown below.  
![][image24]

Each cluster is associated with a label A, B, C, and D.

For K-Means clustering:  
![][image25]

For Hierarchical Clustering:  
![][image26]  
Cluster A represents credit card users that have low activity, cluster B represents high spenders and active credit card users, cluster C represents credit card users with high balances and cash advances, whereas cluster D represents low to moderate activity credit card users.

**Cluster Centers/Profiles**  
Because the dataset used has 14 features, listing out each feature’s average value for each cluster would take too much space. Therefore, if the google colab is opened, cell 31 shows a table showing the average feature value per cluster, and cell 32 shows a comparison of those averages to the overall mean values for K-Means clustering. Similarly, cell 46 shows a table displaying the average feature values per cluster, and cell 47 shows a comparison of those averages against the overall mean values for hierarchical clustering.

K-Means Scatterplot:  
 ![][image27]

Hierarchical Scatterplot:  
![][image22]

**Validation**  
Because there are no labels, no comparison can be made between the clusters and the true labels. The identified clusters align well with realistic credit card user segments based on domain knowledge. For example, groups such as high spenders, low activity users, and credit-dependent users were clearly observed based on their data. Based on their traits and characteristics, they were plotted on different sections of the scatterplot and grouped differently. For example, some users showed both high spending and high payment behavior, indicating financially strong but highly active customers. Those data points were plotted on the farthest right side of the scatterplot. Additionally, outliers contributed to meaningful cluster distinctions, supporting the decision to retain them rather than ignore them or exclude them from the analysis.

**Findings & Conclusion**

**Key Findings**  
The clustering analysis using K-Means and Hierarchical clustering revealed several meaningful insights into customer behavior. First, distinct groups such as high spenders, low activity users, credit-dependent users, and financially responsible users were clearly identified, showing that customer behavior varied significantly across the dataset. Second, features such as PURCHASES, CASH\_ADVANCE, BALANCE, and PRC\_FULL\_PAYMENT played a major role in forming clusters, as they displayed a diverse range of customer behaviors. Third, high spenders tended to have higher credit limits and transaction frequencies, while credit-dependent users showed higher balances and cash advance usage with lower full payment rates. Fourth, low activity users consistently had low values across most features, indicating minimal engagement. Lastly, outliers contributed to meaningful cluster distinctions rather than noise, reinforcing their importance in identifying extreme user behaviors. Overall, hierarchical clustering performed best due to its higher silhouette score and ability to better capture the natural structure of the data, making it more effective for this dataset.

**Conclusion**  
This project successfully applied clustering techniques to identify meaningful groups of credit card users based on their financial behavior. The analysis revealed distinct segments such as high spenders, low activity users, credit-dependent users, and responsible users, which can be used by financial industries to better suit their assets towards different customers’ needs and behaviors. Among the methods tested, hierarchical clustering is recommended due to its higher silhouette score and stronger ability to capture the natural structure of the data. Overall, the project demonstrates how clustering can provide valuable insights for data-driven decision-making in the financial industry.

**Future Work**  
Several improvements can be made to further enhance the accuracy and efficiency of this analysis. First, additional data such as customer demographics or credit history could be incorporated to provide deeper insights and improve cluster interpretation. Second, different, more complex clustering algorithms other than hierarchical and K-Means clustering can be used to more accurately segment the data points into clusters. Lastly, implementing hyperparameter tuning, including experimenting with different linkage methods for hierarchical clustering or optimizing k values more extensively, could improve results.
